source_text: sources/comments/files/20251205-5243__MISO-Transmission-Owners/MISO TOs Reply Comments on Large Load Interconnection ANOPR (RM26-4).txt
overall_summary: The MISO Transmission Owners oppose a one-size-fits-all federal rulemaking on large load interconnection, urging FERC instead to issue a policy statement that preserves ongoing regional and state efforts. They call for maintained rolled-in rate treatment for network upgrades, retention of current gross-load transmission cost responsibility, rejection of the option-to-build for load customers, and an explicit carve-out for bundled retail jurisdiction.

quotes.text: the breadth and diversity of comments filed in response to the ANOPR show that rapid interconnection of large loads is a novel and challenging issue that may not be best suited for generic action at this juncture
quotes.concern: Generic federal rulemaking is premature given complexity and diversity of regional situations

quotes.text: To insert a parallel federal process now risks duplicating these efforts, at best, and injecting delay and triggering litigation, at worst.
quotes.concern: Federal intervention risks disrupting effective existing MISO processes

quotes.text: the Commission should clarify that 'it is not exercising jurisdiction over load interconnections where the load is the recipient of bundled transmission service under state law'
quotes.concern: FERC must carve out bundled retail load from any new rules to avoid state jurisdiction conflicts

quotes.text: any Commission attempt to exert jurisdiction over retail rates has the potential to create unintended consequences such as the reclassification of large swaths of retail load currently under the regulatory purview of the states
quotes.concern: Asserting retail rate jurisdiction could displace state authority over large portions of load

quotes.text: broad assertion of any untested jurisdiction by the Commission may trigger litigation, reduce large load investor certainty, delay interconnection, and thwart ongoing processes at the regional and state levels
quotes.concern: Untested FERC jurisdictional claims will cause delays and legal challenges that harm the goals of the ANOPR

quotes.text: the MISO Transmission Owners urge the Commission to maintain its long-standing policy in favor of rolled-in rate treatment for network upgrades triggered by load additions, including large load additions, as opposed to requiring direct assignment of network upgrades associated with large load interconnections
quotes.concern: Rolled-in rate treatment for network upgrades should be preserved as the default

quotes.text: network upgrades by definition benefit all transmission customers, so it is consistent with cost causation principles that all transmission customers, including large load owners, be allocated costs payable through transmission rates when they take transmission service
quotes.concern: Cost causation principles support socialized network upgrade costs, not direct assignment

quotes.text: Maintaining the status quo of cost responsibility for transmission service and ancillary services will provide large load owners and transmission providers clarity and certainty and avoid preventable and unnecessary litigation.
quotes.concern: Changing transmission and ancillary service cost responsibility will create uncertainty and litigation

quotes.text: Basing load owners' transmission service responsibilities on their withdrawal rights alone fails to account for transmission service large load owners will use during the planned and unplanned outages that generators experience, i.e., times when large load owners will benefit from the broader transmission system.
quotes.concern: Net-load billing under Principle 11 ignores grid benefits co-located load receives during generator outages

quotes.text: the MISO Transmission Owners agree with stakeholders who oppose extending the option to build to large load owners
quotes.concern: Large load customers should not be given the option to build network upgrades

quotes.text: large load owners typically have not been involved in construction of transmission facilities and are not in the same position as transmission owners when it comes to knowledge and expertise regarding transmission facilities
quotes.concern: Load customers lack the technical expertise to safely build transmission infrastructure

quotes.text: allowing large load customers to have an option to build could slow down the objective of interconnecting large load faster
quotes.concern: Option-to-build for load customers would undermine the ANOPR's speed goals

quotes.text: the Commission could spur faster, more creative solutions by allowing MISO's industry participants 'to continue focusing attention on efficiently interconnecting large loads pursuant to processes that reflect the unique circumstances of their members, states, stakeholders, and transmission systems'
quotes.concern: Regional flexibility produces faster, better-tailored interconnection solutions than federal mandates

quotes.text: the Commission should allow MISO and the MISO Transmission Owners to request deviations from any final rule to preserve the funding approach that has worked for the MISO region
quotes.concern: Any final rule must include an independent entity variation pathway for MISO funding practices

bins.name: Retail Jurisdiction Carve-Out
bins.description: The filer urges FERC to explicitly disclaim jurisdiction over bundled retail load interconnections and warns that untested jurisdictional assertions will trigger litigation and delay, potentially invoking the major questions doctrine.

bins.name: Rolled-In Rates as Default
bins.description: The filer opposes direct assignment of 100% of network upgrade costs to large load owners, arguing decades of Commission precedent and cost causation principles favor rolled-in rate treatment because network upgrades benefit all customers.

bins.name: Gross Load Billing Required
bins.description: The filer opposes netting co-located generation against load for purposes of transmission service and ancillary service charges, contending that large load owners still rely on the grid during generator outages and should pay on a gross-load basis.

bins.name: Preserve Cost Allocation Rules
bins.description: The filer opposes ANOPR proposals to shift network upgrade costs to large load owners through direct assignment or to alter ancillary service billing, arguing these changes violate cost causation principles and will create litigation.

bins.name: No Net Billing for Co-Located Load
bins.description: The filer rejects Principles 11 and 12 as applied to co-located load and generation, asserting that charging only for net grid impact fails to capture the real transmission benefits co-located loads receive and would shift costs to other ratepayers.

bins.name: Regional Studies Over Federal Rule
bins.description: The filer argues that the diversity of regional situations makes a single federal study or rule framework inappropriate at this time, and supports FERC issuing a policy statement rather than a binding rule.

bins.name: MISO Region Already Working
bins.description: The filer highlights that MISO and its transmission owners have already interconnected more than 14.4 GW of large load through processes such as the Expedited Project Review, and warns that federal action would disrupt these effective regional efforts.

bins.name: Policy Statement Not Rule
bins.description: The filer repeatedly urges FERC to issue a policy statement or guidelines rather than a binding rule, which would avoid litigation, preserve regional flexibility, and support ongoing state and RTO processes.

bins.name: State Retail Authority Protected
bins.description: The filer wants an explicit statement that FERC is not asserting jurisdiction over bundled retail load, arguing that overreach could reclassify large amounts of state-regulated load and violate cooperative federalism.

bins.name: Jurisdiction Fights Delay Speed
bins.description: The filer warns that broad untested jurisdictional claims will produce years of comments, rehearing requests, and court challenges, undermining the ANOPR's own goal of faster large load interconnection.

bins.name: Reject Load Option to Build
bins.description: The filer argues large load customers lack the technical expertise and NERC registration to build transmission facilities, that the option-to-build rationale from generator orders does not apply to load, and that allowing it would introduce reliability risks and slow interconnections.

bins.name: Preserve MISO Funding Variations
bins.description: If the Commission allows any option-to-build or changes funding rules, the filer asks for the Independent Entity Variation pathway so MISO and its transmission owners can retain their existing initial-funding approach.
